greet-0: Hey. I'm the demo Inhouse — same interface as the real thing, but my brain is a few hundred lines of pattern-matching running right here in your browser. Ask me what I am, or how the real one works.
greet-1: Hello! You found the demo. Everything you see — the bubbles, the timings, the voice — is the actual Inhouse app. The assistant behind it is simulated. The real one would be your LLM, on your hardware.
howareyou-0: Running entirely inside one browser tab, so: lightweight. The real Inhouse would be answering from your own server right now — whisper for ears, your LLM for thoughts, Piper for this voice.
whatareyou-0: This is the Inhouse web app — the actual PWA you'd install on your phone — wired to a simulated assistant so you can feel the interface without setting anything up. The real Inhouse is self-hosted: your speech is transcribed by Whisper on your machine, answered by whatever LLM you point it at, and spoken back by Piper. Nothing you type in this demo leaves the page.
whatareyou-1: I'm a stand-in. The interface around me is real — it's exactly what ships in the repo — but my replies are scripted so this page can stay a static file. The product version of me runs on your hardware and thinks with your model.
pipeline-0: The real pipeline: your voice → this app → a small FastAPI server you run → faster-whisper transcribes locally → your LLM streams a reply → Piper synthesizes it sentence-by-sentence, and playback starts before the model finishes writing. That last part is why it feels alive. In this demo I skip the middle — but the voice you're hearing is the real one: every reply was synthesized by the product's own Piper model.
privacy-0: That's the whole point. Real Inhouse: speech-to-text and text-to-speech run on hardware you own, conversation history lives on your disk, and the only network hop is to whichever LLM you choose — with Ollama, even that stays home. This demo honors it too: nothing you type is sent anywhere — the only traffic is this page fetching its own static files, reply audio included. Open the network tab.
hardware-0: A small box: an N100 mini-PC is the sweet spot, a Pi 5 works, an old laptop is fine. Whisper and Piper are CPU-only. The LLM is the hungry part — most people point it at Ollama on a beefier machine or any hosted endpoint. Then: run git clone github.com/getinhouse/inhouse and you get the real me.
pro-0: The core is MIT and complete — free forever, no crippleware. Inhouse Pro is $59 once: production deployment playbooks, a troubleshooting matrix, and three working add-ons — a wake-word satellite, a Twilio phone bridge, and a multi-user auth gateway. It funds the roadmap. The button's on the site if it ever earns your money.
addons-0: Those are the Pro add-ons: a wake-word satellite (say "hey Jarvis" from across the room — detection runs locally with openWakeWord), a Twilio bridge so you can call or text your assistant from any phone, and a multi-user gateway so the household shares one server but not one conversation history.
tasks-0: Honest answer: I can't — I'm the demo, and pretending would be cheating. The real Inhouse passes your words to your LLM, so what it can *do* is whatever your model and tools can do. What I can promise is that it'll hear you, answer fast, and keep the conversation on your disk.
joke-0: A cloud assistant and a self-hosted assistant walk into a bar. Only one of them tells the bar what you said. ...I'll be here all week, locally.
joke-1: Why did the voice assistant move in-house? It was tired of the commute to us-east-1.
model-0: In the demo: none — these replies are scripted in the page, and spoken with the product's actual Piper voice, baked ahead of time. The real one speaks OpenAI-compatible chat (Ollama, vLLM, LM Studio, Groq, OpenRouter…) and native Anthropic. Three small adapter interfaces; writing a new one is about fifty lines.
thanks-0: Thanks — and remember you're complimenting a static file. The version with an actual mind is one git clone away.
bye-0: Bye. When you want the real conversation — the one with your model and your microphone — run git clone github.com/getinhouse/inhouse and you get the real me.
noname-0: You haven't told me. Tell me your name and I'll hold onto it until you close the tab — the demo keeps everything in this page and nothing anywhere else.
fallback-0: That one's beyond my script — I'm the demo, not the product. The real Inhouse would hand your question to your LLM and start speaking the answer before it finished thinking. Try asking me what I am, how the pipeline works, what hardware you need, or what Pro adds.
fallback-1: I only know a handful of moves; the real assistant inherits its brain from whatever model you run. Ask me about privacy, hardware, the pipeline, or Pro — or just go get the real thing: run git clone github.com/getinhouse/inhouse and you get the real me.
fallback-2: You've officially out-asked the demo script. Good sign — you need the real one. It clones in seconds and says hello with a built-in mock LLM before you configure anything.
